questions.text: A player deliberately handles the ball, preventing a goal. The goalkeeper does the same and would otherwise have conceded. How does the law differ in these two cases?
questions.explanation: When a non-goalkeeper player deliberately handles the ball preventing a goal, they receive a red card (denial of a goal by handball). A goalkeeper doing the same inside their own penalty area receives a red card for denying an obvious goal-scoring opportunity, but a penalty is also awarded to the attackers.

options.text: Both are red cards, but the goalkeeper incident also results in a penalty to the attackers
Both result in a penalty; the goalkeeper gets only a yellow card
Only the outfield player is sent off; the goalkeeper is protected by different rules
Both are yellow cards when the handball is in the penalty area

questions.text: A player kicks the ball against the referee, and the ball enters the goal. What is the correct decision?
questions.explanation: Under IFAB 2019+ rules, if the ball strikes the referee and goes into the goal, play is stopped and a dropped ball is given to the goalkeeper of the team that last touched the ball. The goal does not stand.

options.text: The goal is disallowed; a dropped ball is given to the goalkeeper
The goal stands because the referee is part of the field of play
A corner kick is awarded to the attacking team
An indirect free kick is given to the defending team

questions.text: During a penalty shootout, a goalkeeper moves off their line early and the kick is missed. What does the referee award?
questions.explanation: If the goalkeeper moves off the line before the kick is taken and the kick is missed, the referee must caution the goalkeeper and order a retake — provided the encroachment was by the goalkeeper and no teammate encroached as well.

options.text: A retake is ordered and the goalkeeper is cautioned
The kick stands as a miss — no retake is awarded
The goal is awarded to the attacking team
The goalkeeper is sent off and a retake is ordered

questions.text: A player is in their own half but plays a through ball to a teammate who is level with the last defender in the attacking half. Is the receiving player offside?
questions.explanation: A player is only in an offside position if any part of the body that can play the ball is nearer to the opponents' goal line than the second-to-last opponent. Being level with the last defender means the player is NOT in an offside position.

options.text: No — being level with the last defender means not offside
Yes — the player must be behind the last defender
It depends on which foot the pass was made with
Yes — being level counts as offside under current IFAB rules

questions.text: A player taking a direct free kick touches the ball a second time before any other player. What is the restart?
questions.explanation: If the player who takes a free kick touches the ball a second time before any other player has touched it, an indirect free kick is awarded to the opposing team from where the infringement occurred.

options.text: An indirect free kick to the opposing team
The goal is awarded if the ball crossed the line
A direct free kick to the opposing team
Play continues — double touch is only illegal from a penalty kick

questions.text: Which of the following handball situations does NOT automatically result in a direct free kick or penalty under current IFAB rules?
questions.explanation: Accidental handball by an attacker immediately before a teammate scores does result in the goal being disallowed, but accidental handball by a defender is only penalised if the ball was played directly towards goal or created a goal-scoring opportunity. Handball while falling where the arm is close to the body is typically not an offence.

options.text: Accidental contact with the arm held close to the body while falling
A defender's arm extending unnaturally when struck by the ball
A goal scored directly via a player's hand or arm
An attacker handling the ball immediately before scoring
